content:
8 min read
Just now

--

You’re running a large language model locally. You want speed with limited VRAM, as most of the community suggested, you pick Q4 quantization — four bits per weight instead of eight. Less data means less memory bandwidth, right? It should be faster.
It’s not.
At least, not always. And when you’re running models like Qwen3.6–27B on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090, Q8_0 can actually beat both Q4_K_M and Q6_K_XL in raw prompt processing speed. I know what you’re thinking: That makes no sense.
But it does. Once you understand how quantized weights are stored on disk, how llama.cpp unpacks them during inference, and where the real bottleneck lives on your GPU hardware, the counterintuitive becomes obvious.
Let me take you there — from the GGUF file format all the way to CUDA kernel execution.
1. Two RTX 3090s Running Qwen3.6–27B with Real Benchmark Data
Here’s my test environment for this investigation:
| Component | Specification |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| GPU | 2× NVIDIA RTX 3090 (24 GB) |
| VRAM Total | 48 GB |
| Memory Bandwidth | ~936 GB/s per card |
| Model | Qwen3.6-27B GGUF |
| Backend | llama.cpp with CUDA |
| Quantizations | Q8_0, Q6_K_XL, Q4_K_M |
